rawIntro.context: Germanium · Raw Material · Supply chain overview
rawIntro.thesis: Germanium is never mined directly — it is captured as a trace byproduct of coal combustion and zinc smelting, with ~83% of global refined supply concentrated in Chinese-controlled operations.
rawIntro.title: The invisible chokepoint
rawIntro.sub: ~230 t/yr global supply · $1.4B market · no substitute

sections.label: Germanium is never mined directly
sections.content: There is no such thing as a germanium mine. Germanium exists only as a trace impurity inside zinc sulfide ores and certain lignite coal seams. Germanium recovery is always an afterthought layered on top of a primary industrial process — zinc smelting or coal combustion.

sections.label: The capture problem
sections.content: Of all the germanium that theoretically passes through the world's coal plants and zinc smelters each year, only approximately 17% is actually captured. At Wulantuga alone, ~137 tonnes passes through the coal burned annually — but only 20-35 tonnes is recovered. Recovery requires specialist acid-leach circuits that cost millions to install and are only economic when germanium prices are high enough.

sections.label: Geographic concentration
content: China controls ~80% of primary refined germanium globally and ~100% of all ground-source supply.
Three deposits account for essentially all current global production. There are no western deposits of consequence currently in production.
China introduced export licensing in August 2023 — exports fell ~55%. In December 2024, China banned Ge exports to the US entirely.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: The most actionable western plays are companies with secured non-Chinese Ge supply or processing capacity: Umicore (UMI.BR) as the primary western refiner; 5N Plus (VFF.TSX) as the only vertically integrated western wafer-to-space-solar-cell producer.

brief.text: There are 8 deposits where germanium concentrate can be found, hosted inside coal or zinc reserves — never as a standalone mineral. Five are in China and subject to export controls. One is in Russia, blocked from western markets by sanctions since 2022. The remaining two are Big Hill in the DRC — only beginning to ship concentrate to Umicore under a multi-year exclusive agreement — and Red Dog in Alaska, whose mine life ends in 2031 with no identified replacement. When Red Dog closes, the only non-Chinese, non-Russian primary deposit with meaningful throughput disappears from the western supply picture entirely.
brief.summary: 8 deposits globally — 5 in China, 1 Russia-sanctioned, 1 closing 2031, 1 just beginning to ramp in DRC.

brief.text: Miners capture germanium either from coal fly ash or through zinc smelting — it is always a byproduct, never the reason the mine exists. Only ~17% of the germanium passing through these industrial processes is actually captured. The rest is lost to slag and waste. Increasing recovery would require installing acid-leach circuits costing millions in capex — equipment miners will not invest in unless germanium prices stay elevated long enough to justify the spend. Even then, the build-out takes years. Primary supply is therefore fixed by industrial throughput, not by investment decisions. You cannot choose to produce more germanium the way you can choose to drill more oil wells.
brief.summary: Germanium is always a byproduct — only ~17% of what passes through industrial processes is captured.

brief.text: Germanium is refined typically by the same operator that extracts it. The majority of refining capacity is in China. Russia's JSC Germanium — historically a meaningful western supplier — is cut off by sanctions. Umicore in Belgium is the only western refiner operating at scale, sourcing from three streams: fiber manufacturing scrap from Corning, Prysmian, and Fujikura (~50%); zinc smelting byproduct concentrate (~35%); and DRC concentrate from the Big Hill partnership, ramping since October 2024 (~15%, growing). 5N Plus in Canada operates a smaller closed-loop model with US DoD backing in Utah. Both run tolling agreements — they sell GeCl₄ to fiber manufacturers, take back production scrap, and refine it back into GeCl₄. The recycling loop is structurally self-limiting: if Chinese export controls reduce western fiber production, the scrap stream feeding western recyclers shrinks simultaneously.
brief.summary: One western refiner at scale (Umicore). The recycling loop is structurally self-limiting.

brief.text: Refined and recycled output totals an estimated ~220 tonnes per year. But ~120t of that is Chinese primary supply — subject to export controls that have already tightened twice since 2023 and expire on a suspension basis in November 2026. Another ~20t is Russian, inaccessible to western buyers. That leaves roughly ~80t of genuinely western-accessible supply from Umicore, 5N Plus, and PPM Pure Metals combined — against an AI datacenter fiber buildout that will require ~325t of germanium by 2026. The gap is not a future risk. It is already open.
brief.summary: ~80t western-accessible supply against ~325t of AI datacenter demand by 2026 — the gap is already open.

compIntro.thesis: ~90 tonnes of germanium — 40% of global supply — is converted to ultrapure GeCl₄ and deposited into silica glass by six fiber manufacturers. Their combined output of ~500M fiber-km/yr must nearly triple to meet AI datacenter demand by 2026.
compIntro.title: Where germanium becomes glass
compIntro.sub: GeO₂/GeCl₄ → preform → fiber strand · Ge locked permanently

sections.label: The transformation
sections.content: When germanium enters the component layer, it undergoes its most fundamental transformation. GeO₂ powder or GeCl₄ vapor is incorporated into a silica glass matrix during preform manufacturing. From this point forward, the germanium is no longer a commodity — it is permanently locked into the glass structure.

sections.label: Why Ge is irreplaceable here
sections.content: The refractive index of pure silica glass is approximately 1.444. Adding germanium dioxide raises it to ~1.468 in the core. This precise gradient — higher in the center, lower at the cladding — causes total internal reflection, confining light to the fiber core. No other dopant achieves the same combination of refractive index increase, low attenuation at telecom wavelengths, and compatibility with the SiO₂ deposition process at industrial scale.

sections.label: Market concentration
content: Six companies control essentially all preform manufacturing globally: Corning, Prysmian, Fujikura, Sumitomo, Shin-Etsu, and YOFC.
YOFC (Chinese state-backed, partial Corning JV) has the largest single capacity globally.
AI datacenter buildout has created multi-year backlogs at all major western producers.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Corning (GLW) is the most liquid and direct western proxy for this layer. Prysmian (PRY) adds subsea cable exposure. Both benefit from a structural supply constraint upstream and a structural demand surge downstream.

brief.text: Ultrapure germanium tetrachloride — GeCl₄ — is the chemical form germanium must take before it can be deposited into fiber optic preform. Umicore is the sole western supplier of GeCl₄ at semiconductor grade (8N purity). All other significant suppliers are in China or Russia. Every western fiber manufacturer — Corning, Prysmian, Fujikura, Sumitomo — depends on a single Belgian company for their germanium input. There is no western alternative. Chinese and Russian GeCl₄ suppliers serve their domestic manufacturers only, and are either export-controlled or sanctioned.
brief.summary: Umicore in Belgium is the sole western supplier of GeCl₄ at semiconductor grade — there is no alternative.

brief.text: Six companies produce the overwhelming majority of the world's optical fiber preform and strand. Glass is not the constraint — silica is abundant. The constraint is the CVD deposition equipment that builds germanium-doped glass rods to parts-per-billion purity. This equipment takes 18-24 months to commission, runs at 100% global utilization, and cannot be rushed. Corning is building a $1B preform plant in Arizona. Prysmian acquired North American preform capacity in 2024. Industry expansion projects will add 55-90M km of new capacity — but earliest arrival is late 2027, and this is modest against 1.3B km/yr of AI demand.
brief.summary: CVD deposition equipment runs at 100% global utilization — cannot be rushed, new capacity earliest late 2027.

brief.text: Current global optical fiber production is ~650M fiber-km per year. AI datacenter buildout is driving demand toward ~1.3B fiber-km per year by 2026 — a near-doubling requiring a proportional increase in GeCl₄ supply that the western supply chain cannot provide. At 100% preform utilization globally, single-mode fiber prices have risen +500% since January 2025. At least one major US manufacturer is sold out through 2026.
brief.summary: Fiber prices up +500% since Jan 2025. At least one major US manufacturer sold out through 2026.

subIntro.thesis: Six cable assemblers and three submarine specialists take germanium-doped fiber and deploy it as the physical infrastructure of the internet — at a moment when AI datacenter demand is requiring 36× more fiber per rack than anything built before, against a supply chain running at 100% capacity.
subIntro.title: From strand to deployable cable
subIntro.sub: Assembly · 3 cable types · one deployable product

sections.label: The transformation
sections.content: The subsystem layer is where individual fiber strands are bundled, protected, and structured into a deployable cable product. Germanium is already permanently locked in. No new Ge is added at this layer. The transformation is mechanical — ribbonizing, buffering, jacketing, armoring, and connector termination.

sections.label: Three cable types, one product family
sections.content: The same fundamental technology — germanium-doped glass fiber — underlies three distinct cable specifications: datacenter cable optimized for fiber density in short runs, terrestrial long-haul cable armored for outdoor deployment, and subsea cable engineered to survive ocean floor conditions for 25+ years.

sections.label: Market dynamics
content: AI hyperscalers are driving unprecedented demand for datacenter cable — Corning has guided for multi-year order visibility for the first time in company history.
The same hyperscalers (Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon) are funding private subsea cables directly, shifting demand from carrier-funded to tech-funded.
Cable manufacturing capacity is now the bottleneck — not fiber strand or Ge supply.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Corning (GLW) and Prysmian (PRY) capture the most value at this layer. Prysmian additionally captures the subsea boom via Alcatel Submarine Networks. The Ge supply constraint upstream is now a competitive advantage for companies that have secured alternative Ge supply.

brief.text: Cable assembly is largely commoditized. Most preform manufacturers are vertically integrated through to cable — Corning makes preform, draws fiber, and assembles cable in the same facilities. The same companies that appear in the component layer reappear here. The subsystem layer leads the datacenter buildout cycle rather than following it — hyperscalers order cable 12-18 months before a facility opens, meaning today's order books reflect 2027 demand.
brief.summary: Vertically integrated — the same companies appear in both the component and subsystem layers.

brief.text: Three distinct cable categories serve different parts of the AI infrastructure buildout. Datacenter cable connects GPU racks within facilities — this is where the 36× fiber multiplier per AI rack versus CPU rack shows up most acutely. Terrestrial long-haul connects facilities to the broader internet backbone. Subsea cable connects continents — a specialized market with only ~60 cable ships globally, a fleet that cannot be quickly expanded and is already booked years forward.
brief.summary: Three cable categories: datacenter, terrestrial long-haul, subsea — each with a distinct bottleneck.

brief.text: The constraint at this layer is logistical rather than material. Specialized installation labor is scarce. Conduit permitting in dense urban areas takes years. For subsea cable, the global fleet of ~60 vessels represents a hard physical limit on how much new capacity can be laid per year — and $3B in new vessel construction would be needed to meaningfully expand it. Hyperscaler private fiber — Meta, Google, Microsoft building their own networks rather than leasing from carriers — is consuming an increasing share of both cable production and installation capacity.
brief.summary: ~60 cable ships globally is a hard physical ceiling. Hyperscaler private fiber is consuming installation capacity.

briefStats.label: More fiber per AI rack vs CPU rack

euIntro.thesis: AI datacenters are the terminal node of the germanium supply chain — every GPU-to-GPU fiber connection began as germanium dioxide in a Chinese coal seam or zinc mine.
euIntro.title: Where the chain ends

sections.label: The terminus
sections.content: The AI datacenter is where the entire germanium supply chain terminates. Every fiber optic cable connecting GPU to GPU, switch to switch, and datacenter to datacenter began as germanium dioxide in a Chinese coal seam or zinc mine. A single hyperscale AI training cluster contains millions of kilometers of fiber — representing thousands of kilograms of germanium permanently embedded in glass.

sections.label: The density inflection
sections.content: Traditional datacenter racks consumed 5-10 kilowatts. An AI GPU rack running H100s consumes 40-100 kilowatts — a 10-20x density increase. This creates cascading demand: more power infrastructure, more cooling, more fiber for interconnect, and more of every upstream input including germanium.

sections.label: Infrastructure investment wave
content: Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta have collectively committed over $250B in datacenter capex for 2025 alone — the largest single-year infrastructure investment in history.
Power grid constraints are now the primary bottleneck for datacenter growth in key markets (Virginia, Ireland, Singapore) — not compute or fiber supply.
The US Stargate initiative ($500B over 4 years) represents a government-backed guarantee of sustained AI infrastructure demand.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: At the end use layer, the most direct investment plays are the infrastructure enablers (Vertiv, Schneider Electric) rather than the hyperscalers themselves. Vertiv in particular has the most concentrated AI datacenter revenue exposure of any large-cap public company. Among hyperscalers, Microsoft offers the clearest AI revenue visibility through Azure OpenAI. Oracle is the most underappreciated — OCI is growing faster than any major cloud platform driven entirely by AI demand.

brief.text: Fiber installation contractors are the execution layer for all AI infrastructure deployment. Every kilometer of fiber that hyperscalers order has to be physically pulled, spliced, and tested by a specialized labor force. Dycom, MasTec, and their peers are structurally under-covered as AI infrastructure beneficiaries — they are priced as construction contractors but are the indispensable bottleneck between capital commitment and deployed capacity.
brief.summary: Fiber contractors are the indispensable bottleneck between committed hyperscaler capex and deployed capacity.

brief.text: Datacenter developers translate hyperscaler capex commitments into physical facilities. Turner Construction, Mortenson, and DPR are building the majority of US hyperscale facilities. Most major hyperscalers also have significant self-build programs — $500B+ in announced capex for 2026 — which means the developer layer is increasingly bifurcated between contracted specialists and in-house teams.
brief.summary: $500B+ in hyperscaler self-build capex for 2026 — developer capacity is fully allocated.

brief.text: $600B in hyperscaler capex in 2026. 130-140 new hyperscale facilities per year. Each AI GPU rack requires 36× more fiber than a CPU rack — not an incremental increase but a structural step change in per-unit demand. Power constraints — the primary bottleneck blocking facility approvals — are being resolved through nuclear PPAs, dedicated gas generation, and large-scale solar. As blocked projects unblock, fiber demand accelerates further. Sovereign wealth funds from the GCC — G42 (UAE) and NEOM/PIF (Saudi Arabia) — are now building hyperscale capacity independently, extending demand beyond the traditional US hyperscaler base.
brief.summary: $600B capex in 2026. GCC sovereign funds (G42, NEOM/PIF) now building hyperscale capacity independently.

briefStats.label: Fiber per AI rack vs CPU rack
Ge needed for AI DCs alone by 2026

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: The integration layer sits between the fiber optic cable and the hyperscale operators. These companies provide the power infrastructure, thermal management, colocation space, structured cabling, and physical construction that transform an empty building into a functioning AI datacenter.

sections.label: Why this layer matters
sections.content: AI GPU clusters require 10-20x more power density than traditional compute. This has created extraordinary demand for power distribution systems, liquid cooling infrastructure, and the engineering expertise to design facilities capable of handling these loads. Vertiv and Schneider Electric are the primary beneficiaries.

sections.label: Key risks
content: Power transformer lead times have extended to 2-3 years — the integration layer is itself supply-constrained.
Labor shortages in datacenter construction are extending project timelines across the industry.
Hyperscalers are increasingly in-sourcing integration work, reducing the addressable market for third-party integrators.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Vertiv (VRT) is the highest-conviction AI infrastructure play at this layer — pure-play power and cooling with >20% revenue growth. Schneider Electric (SU) offers broader industrial exposure with meaningful datacenter upside. AECOM (ACM) is the most indirect play — construction management of hyperscale campuses.

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: Six companies control the vast majority of AI compute infrastructure globally: Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta, xAI, and Oracle. Together they are committing over $250B in datacenter capex in 2025 — the largest single-year infrastructure investment in history.

sections.label: Concentration
sections.content: The hyperscale layer is extraordinarily concentrated. The top four operators (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) account for over 80% of all AI compute capacity globally. This concentration means that decisions by a handful of executives determine the demand for the entire Ge-to-fiber supply chain upstream.

sections.label: Key risks
content: AI model efficiency improvements (smaller models achieving GPT-4 capability) could reduce training compute demand.
Regulatory scrutiny of hyperscaler AI dominance is intensifying in the US, EU, and UK.
Power grid constraints in Virginia, Ireland, and Singapore are already limiting datacenter expansion in key markets.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Microsoft (MSFT) offers the clearest AI revenue visibility through Azure OpenAI. Google (GOOGL) offers AI exposure at the lowest valuation multiple among hyperscalers plus TPU custom silicon upside. Oracle (ORCL) is the fastest-growing cloud platform and most underappreciated AI infrastructure play. xAI is private — monitor for IPO.

OUTPUT_EU.title: AI Datacenter output
OUTPUT_EU.sub: The terminus of the Ge supply chain

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: The AI Datacenter is the final node in the germanium supply chain. Every fiber optic cable — every strand of germanium-doped glass — ultimately terminates here, connecting GPU to GPU, rack to rack, datacenter to datacenter.

sections.label: Scale
sections.content: A single hyperscale AI training cluster may contain 50,000-100,000 GPUs connected by millions of kilometers of fiber. The global AI datacenter buildout represents the largest single deployment of optical fiber in history — and therefore the largest single drawdown on germanium supply.

sections.label: Key risks
content: Power grid constraints are the primary bottleneck — not compute, fiber, or Ge supply.
AI model efficiency could reduce training compute requirements faster than demand grows.
Geopolitical risk: Chinese Ge export controls create structural cost headwinds for all western AI infrastructure.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: At the output level, the value of AI compute accrues to the model operators and the applications built on top of them. The infrastructure supply chain — including the Ge-to-fiber chain mapped here — is the hidden upstream leverage on the entire AI economy.

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: All economically significant germanium deposits in production are in China. Three deposits — Lincang, Wulantuga, and Huize/SYGT — account for essentially all current global primary Ge production.

sections.label: Geological risk
content: Ge is never mined directly — it exists only as a trace impurity. Supply cannot be ramped independently of zinc or coal production decisions.
The highest-grade deposits are depleting assets. No comparable replacements have been identified outside China.
Theoretical reserves significantly exceed recoverable reserves.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Deposit-level exposure is only accessible through Chinese listed equities. No western-listed junior miner has a credible Ge deposit in development.

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: All active germanium miners are Chinese. Two are listed domestically; two are state-owned enterprises. Mining decisions are made for zinc and coal economics, not Ge demand.

sections.label: Key risks
content: A zinc or coal downturn reduces Ge output regardless of Ge prices.
State ownership means supply can be withheld as a strategic tool.
No western capacity to replace Chinese mining — a new mine takes 7-10 years.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Lincang Xinyuan (002428.SZ) is the only pure-play Ge miner. Yunnan Chihong (600497.SH) offers Ge exposure as a high-margin add-on to a zinc business.

REFINERS.title: Refiner layer
REFINERS.sub: 3 refiners · China dominant · Umicore western alternative

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: The refining layer is where Ge concentrate becomes the high-purity GeO₂, GeCl₄, and Ge wafers the fiber optic and solar industries require. China controls ~80% of primary refining capacity.

sections.label: Key risks
content: Chinese refiners produce ~80% of global primary refined Ge. Export controls throttle supply at this exact layer.
Western refining capacity is insufficient without Chinese supply — the gap is structural, not cyclical.
Recycling provides ~90 t/yr globally but cannot grow faster than cumulative historical Ge consumption.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Umicore (UMI.BR) is the most accessible western refining play. 5N Plus (VFF.TSX) is vertically integrated from Ge wafer through to space solar cell.

sections.label: Supply picture
sections.content: Global germanium supply is approximately 230 tonnes per year: ~120 t Chinese primary, ~14 t smaller Chinese refiners, and ~90 t global recycling. The $1.4B market is tiny relative to the technology ecosystems it enables.

sections.label: Structural constraints
content: No short-term supply response possible. New western Ge recovery capacity takes 3-5 years to build.
Recycling cannot grow faster than cumulative historical Ge consumption.
The nearest western alternative adds only ~30 t/yr.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: The supply constraint is durable, not cyclical. Invest in western processing companies, recyclers, and non-Chinese miners with credible development-stage assets.

PREFORM.title: Preform manufacturing
PREFORM.sub: 6 producers · Ge baked permanently into glass core

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: Preform manufacturing is where germanium permanently enters the optical fiber. GeCl₄ vapor or GeO₂ powder is incorporated into a silica glass rod using chemical vapor deposition. One preform rod yields approximately 5,000-10,000 km of finished fiber.

sections.label: Concentration risk
content: Six companies control essentially all global preform capacity. Entry barriers are extremely high.
YOFC (Chinese, partial Corning JV) has the largest single capacity globally.
AI datacenter demand has created unprecedented backlogs — Corning has multi-year demand visibility for the first time.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Corning (GLW) and Prysmian (PRY) are the most accessible listed equities at this layer. Both have significant AI datacenter exposure and benefit from the Ge supply squeeze.

DRAWING.title: Fiber drawing
DRAWING.sub: Preform rod → thousands of km of hair-thin fiber

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: Fiber drawing is the process of heating one end of a preform rod to ~2,000°C and pulling it downward to create a continuous fiber ~125 microns in diameter. The Ge content is now permanently fixed — the drawing process does not alter its chemistry.

sections.label: Vertical integration
sections.content: Drawing is almost entirely vertically integrated — the same companies that make preforms draw their own fiber. Preform manufacturing capacity is the true constraint, not drawing capacity.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: The drawing layer does not introduce new investment theses beyond what is captured at the preform layer. Focus on preform manufacturers.

OUTPUT.title: Optical fiber strand output
OUTPUT.sub: Commodity product · Ge permanently locked · feeds cable assembly

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: The optical fiber strand is the output of the component layer. At this point germanium is no longer identifiable as a separate material — it is simply part of the glass with a specific refractive index profile. The strand is sold by the kilometer at commodity prices.

sections.label: Market dynamics
sections.content: Fiber strand pricing has generally declined per kilometer over decades as manufacturing efficiency improved. The current AI datacenter buildout is creating a demand surge that is outpacing capacity additions for the first time in 15 years.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Do not invest at the strand level. Invest upstream at the preform level or downstream at the cable assembly and hyperscale operator level.

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: Cable assembly is where fiber strands are mechanically transformed into finished cable products. Integrated producers (Corning, Prysmian, Fujikura, Sumitomo) use their own drawn fiber. Cable-only assemblers (CommScope, AFL) buy strand on the open market. Subsea specialists (Alcatel SN, SubCom, NEC) operate an entirely different manufacturing process.

sections.label: Key risks
content: Cable manufacturing capacity is now the bottleneck — not fiber strand or Ge supply. The constraint has shifted downstream.
Subsea cable laying capacity is extremely constrained — only a handful of specialized ships exist globally.
Chinese cable producers have very large capacity and are aggressively pricing in accessible markets.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Corning (GLW) and Prysmian (PRY) are the primary investment vehicles. For subsea exposure specifically, Nokia (NOKIA HE) via Alcatel SN is the only listed vehicle.

CABLE TYPE.title: Cable type layer
CABLE TYPE.sub: Datacenter · Terrestrial · Subsea · same fiber, different spec

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: The three cable types represent the same fundamental product — germanium-doped fiber in a protective structure — engineered for radically different deployment environments.

sections.label: Fastest growing: datacenter
sections.content: Datacenter trunk cable is the fastest-growing segment. AI training clusters require extraordinarily high fiber density — a single hyperscale AI datacenter may contain more fiber than an entire city's telecom network.

sections.label: Key risks
content: Datacenter cable is becoming commoditized as Chinese manufacturers scale.
Subsea cable routes face increasing geopolitical risk.
Terrestrial long-haul demand is more cyclical, tied to telecom operator capital budgets.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: Invest in manufacturers with the highest mix of datacenter and subsea cable revenue — Corning for datacenter, Prysmian for subsea.

OUTPUT_SUB.title: Fiber Optic Cable output
OUTPUT_SUB.sub: Unified deployable product · feeds End Use layer

sections.label: Overview
sections.content: Fiber Optic Cable is the unified subsystem output — the fully manufactured, ready-to-deploy cable. This is the handoff point to the End Use layer. Germanium is irreversibly embedded. The $15B cable market is growing toward $28B by 2030.

sections.label: Market scale
sections.content: Approximately 500 million km of optical fiber is deployed globally. AI-driven demand is adding 50-60 million km per year in new datacenter deployments alone.

sections.label: Investment angle
sections.content: At the output level, invest in the deployers of cable — the hyperscale operators whose AI infrastructure depends on it — rather than in cable as a commodity.
